title: Advanced Footer, Roboto Headings, Hero Theme Settings & Particle Effects

requirements.summary: Redesign Footer component with multi-column layout, social links, and visual consistency
acceptanceCriteria: Footer renders a multi-column layout (branding, navigation, contact, social)
Footer uses the same color palette, spacing, and border/shadow styles as the rest of the UI
A bottom bar with copyright text and a horizontal divider is present
Social media icons are displayed and link correctly
Footer is fully responsive (stacks columns on mobile)

file_operations.description: Redesign Footer to include a multi-column layout with four distinct sections: branding/bio column (doctor name, short tagline), quick navigation links column (smooth-scroll links to Home, About, Services, Clinics, Social sections), contact/clinic info column (phone, email, address if applicable), and social media links column. Add a bottom bar below the columns with a horizontal divider and copyright text. Match the warm neutral tones (OKLCH colors from tailwind.config.js), teal accents, card-based aesthetic (border, shadow, rounded corners) used in Header and other section components. Ensure responsive design where columns stack vertically on mobile. Fetch content and social links from useGetAllContent hook and wire the admin-editable footer description into the branding column.

requirements.summary: Import Roboto font and apply it to all headings across the application
acceptanceCriteria: Roboto font is loaded from Google Fonts
All h1–h6 elements and explicit heading/title classes use Roboto
Tailwind config reflects Roboto in the font family configuration
No heading in the app falls back to a non-Roboto font when Roboto is available

file_operations.description: Add a Google Fonts link tag in the <head> section to load the Roboto font family (multiple weights: 400, 500, 700). Place it alongside the existing Space Grotesk and Inter font imports.

Import Roboto from Google Fonts using @import if not already imported via HTML, and define a global CSS rule targeting all heading elements (h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6) to set font-family: 'Roboto', sans-serif. Ensure this rule has sufficient specificity to override default Tailwind typography.

Update the theme.extend.fontFamily configuration to add a 'heading' or 'display' font stack with Roboto as the primary font. Reference this custom font family in components that render headings. Ensure the existing fontFamily.sans (Inter) and fontFamily.mono (Space Grotesk) remain unchanged, so body text continues to use Inter while headings use Roboto.
Apply the Roboto heading font class (e.g., font-heading or explicit font-['Roboto']) to the site title text element in the header. Verify that the doctor name/site title displays in Roboto.

Apply the Roboto heading font class to the hero section's main title (EditableField for siteTitle) and any other heading elements in the hero. Ensure the tagline or subtitle also uses Roboto if it is styled as a heading.
Apply the Roboto heading font class to the 'About Me' section title and any credential card headings. Ensure all h2/h3 elements in the About page use Roboto.
Apply the Roboto heading font class to the 'What I Offer' section title and individual service card titles. Ensure all service headings render in Roboto.
Apply the Roboto heading font class to the 'Our Clinics' section title and individual clinic card headings. Ensure all clinic name headings use Roboto.
Apply the Roboto heading font class to the 'Follow & Connect' section title. Ensure the social media section heading uses Roboto.
Apply the Roboto heading font class to the footer's branding/bio column heading (doctor name or site title) and any column subheadings. Ensure footer headings use Roboto for visual consistency.

requirements.summary: Expand HeroSettingsEditor with comprehensive theme controls and live preview
acceptanceCriteria: HeroSettingsEditor displays all new controls: gradient pickers, overlay opacity, text color, blur toggle, glassmorphism slider, height selector, animation speed, mouse parallax toggle
A live preview thumbnail reflects the current settings combination
All settings are persisted to the backend and reloaded on page load
The Hero section on the public page correctly applies all saved theme settings
Controls are grouped logically with clear labels and sections

file_operations.description: Expand the HeroSettingsEditor component to include new UI controls: two color pickers for bgGradientStart and bgGradientEnd, a slider for overlayOpacity (0–100%), a color picker for textColor, a toggle for backgroundBlur, a slider for glassmorphismIntensity (0–100%), a dropdown/select for heroHeight (options: 'compact', 'normal', 'full-screen'), a slider for animationSpeed (0–10), and a toggle for mouseParallaxEnabled. Organize these controls into logical sections (Background, Overlay, Text, Effects, Interactions). Add a live preview thumbnail area that renders a miniature version of the hero section reflecting the current settings (use a scaled-down div with inline styles mirroring the gradient, overlay, blur, etc.). Ensure all fields are bound to the IdleHeroSettings type from the backend interface. Use the useUpdateHeroSettings mutation to persist changes. Fetch current settings on mount using useGetHeroSettings and populate the form fields. Verify the component's usage instructions before implementing.

Update the Home/Hero page component to read and apply all hero theme settings from the backend: apply bgGradientStart and bgGradientEnd as a CSS linear-gradient background, apply overlayOpacity as an overlay layer opacity, apply textColor to the title and tagline text, conditionally apply a backdrop-blur class if backgroundBlur is true, apply glassmorphismIntensity as a translucent overlay with varying opacity, apply heroHeight to set the hero section height (e.g., min-h-[50vh] for compact, min-h-screen for full-screen), apply animationSpeed to control CSS animation duration, and conditionally apply mouse parallax effect if mouseParallaxEnabled is true (use mouse move event listeners to adjust background position or element transforms). Fetch hero settings using useGetHeroSettings and apply styles dynamically via inline styles or Tailwind classes. Ensure the hero section updates when settings are saved in the admin panel (use React Query cache invalidation).

requirements.summary: Implement advanced particle effect selection system with presets and fine-tuning controls
acceptanceCriteria: Admin particle settings panel shows a grid of at least 10 named presets with animated preview thumbnails
Selected preset name is saved to hero settings in the backend
Fine-tuning controls (count, size, speed, color, opacity) are present and functional
ParticleCanvas component renders the selected particle preset with fine-tuned parameters on the Hero section
Changing preset in admin updates the live hero section after save
'None' preset disables all particles cleanly

file_operations.description: Add a particle effect selection section to the HeroSettingsEditor. Display a grid of at least 10 named particle presets: 'None', 'Floating Dots', 'Stars', 'Snow', 'Bubbles', 'Geometric Shapes', 'DNA Helix', 'Medical Pulse', 'Confetti', 'Fireflies'. For each preset, render a small card with a label and an animated preview thumbnail (use a mini canvas or CSS animation to show a representative particle animation). Allow the admin to select a preset by clicking on it, updating the particlePreset field in the hero settings. Below the preset grid, add fine-tuning controls: a slider for particleCount (10–500), a min/max range slider for particleMinSize and particleMaxSize (1–50px), a slider for particleSpeed (0.1–10), a color picker for particleColor, and a slider for particleOpacity (0–100%). Ensure all fields are bound to the IdleHeroSettings type. Use the useUpdateHeroSettings mutation to persist the selected preset and fine-tuning parameters. Verify the component's usage instructions before implementing.

Implement the ParticleCanvas component to render client-side particle animations on a canvas element overlaying the hero section. Accept props for the selected particle preset name, particle count, particle size range (min/max), particle speed, particle color, and particle opacity. Use the canvas 2D rendering context or CSS animations to draw particles based on the preset. Implement at least 10 presets: 'None' (render nothing), 'Floating Dots' (circular particles drifting slowly), 'Stars' (twinkling star shapes), 'Snow' (falling snowflakes), 'Bubbles' (rising circular bubbles), 'Geometric Shapes' (rotating triangles/squares), 'DNA Helix' (double-helix spiral pattern), 'Medical Pulse' (pulsing cross/plus symbols), 'Confetti' (falling colorful squares), 'Fireflies' (glowing dots with opacity fade). Use requestAnimationFrame for smooth animation. Apply fine-tuned parameters (count, size, speed, color, opacity) to each particle. Ensure the canvas is positioned absolutely over the hero background and does not block user interaction with the hero content (use pointer-events: none). Fetch particle settings from the hero settings and re-render when settings change. Optimize performance to avoid frame drops with high particle counts.

Wire the ParticleCanvas component into the Home/Hero section. Fetch hero settings using useGetHeroSettings and pass the particle-related fields (particlePreset, particleCount, particleMinSize, particleMaxSize, particleSpeed, particleColor, particleOpacity) as props to ParticleCanvas. Render ParticleCanvas as an overlay layer on top of the hero background but below the hero text content. Ensure the particle canvas spans the full hero section dimensions. If particlePreset is 'None', do not render ParticleCanvas or render it with no particles to avoid unnecessary computation. Ensure the particle effect updates when the admin saves new particle settings (React Query cache invalidation will trigger a re-fetch and re-render).
